---
title: "Lab meeting — review, speaker prompts, term cheat-sheet"
subtitle: "Companion to `lab-meeting-2026-05-14.pptx` (12 slides, v 2026-05-19)"
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: "2026-05-21"
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    geometry: "margin=2.5cm"
    fontsize: 11pt
    mainfont: "STIX Two Text"
    monofont: "Menlo"
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    include-in-header: ../pandoc-header.tex
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
---

# Top-level review

The deck reads well as a narrative arc — *here's the problem → here's how
I worked → here's what broke → here's the result*. A handful of things
I'd consider tightening before you present.

## Issues to fix before the talk

1. **Slide 8 — citation accuracy.** The slide attributes the k₃ typo to
   "Purvis & Diamond 2008", but the audit lab book
   (`lab-book-2026-05-08-sneyd-dufour-audit.md`) records the change as
   `K_IP3R['k3']: 11.0 → 0.11`, an IP3R rate constant, with the audit
   framed around **Sneyd & Dufour 2002**. The "100× too large" fact is
   correct (11.0 → 0.11). Verify which paper actually carried the typo
   before naming it on the slide. Also, the codebase consistently cites
   "Purvis 2008" or "Purvis et al. 2008"; "Purvis & Diamond" reads as a
   two-author paper, which it isn't.

2. **Slide 9 — "arbitrary success criteria".** Drop *arbitrary*. The
   audience will hear it as "I made them up." These are biological-range
   criteria taken from the Dolan & Diamond 2014 validation experiment —
   defined *before* the model was tuned. Suggest **pre-specified** or
   **biological-range** or **Dolan-target**.

3. **Slide 10 — typo.** "Cytoslic" → "Cytosolic".

4. **Slide 1 — title framing.** "The feasibility of a whole-cell
   model" understates what you've already shown. Twenty-one of twenty-one
   tests pass, four resting-state targets hit, paired ± Ca²⁺ behaviour
   reproduced. You've moved past feasibility. Consider "A whole-cell
   model of the human platelet — methodology and first validation"
   or similar. Minor; pick whatever lands for the audience.

## Issues worth thinking about

5. **k₃ story positioning.** In an earlier deck this was the
   methodology punchline (slide 4); here it has been demoted to slide 8
   "An aside". It's actually one of your strongest pieces of evidence
   that *the methodology produces real insight*, not just code — keeping
   it as an "aside" undersells it. Consider re-labelling
   "Aside — but a load-bearing one" or moving it back adjacent to the
   methodology slides.

6. **AI framing on slides 4–6.** At a biology lab meeting, "AI did
   the extraction and the coding" risks the audience hearing "this isn't
   really yours". Tighten the framing to: *AI handled the rote work —
   PDF extraction, code scaffolding, cross-paper consistency. I handled
   the science — validation criteria, biology decisions, accepting or
   rejecting each rate constant.* The k₃ story (slide 8) supports
   exactly that division of labour: AI flagged the anomaly, you verified
   against the primary source.

7. **Order: methodology before model.** Currently the audience has to
   sit through three methodology slides (3, 4, 5, 6) and one aside
   (slide 8) before seeing the full pathway diagram (slide 7) and
   then the results (9–11). Some audiences want *what was built* up
   front. One alternative ordering: 1, 2, **7** (what), 3 (how — short),
   8 (when it earned its keep), 9–11 (results), 12 (outlook). Try it
   on a colleague before committing.

8. **Slide 7 — minor.** "BIP" should be "BiP" (binding immunoglobulin
   protein — lowercase i is the convention). The "(not yet in model)"
   parentheticals are helpful; keep them.

9. **Slide 12 — "Long-recovery cytosolic Ca²⁺ collapse — a limitation
   of modelling perhaps?"** The question mark hedges. You know this is
   a deYoung-Keizer bistability artefact (per memory and lab-book
   trail). Owning it — *"a known limitation of the deYoung-Keizer IP3R
   model under sustained stimulation; fixable in v0.5"* — is stronger
   than asking the question.

10. **No speaker notes in the deck.** Extracted as empty. The cue
    cards below are your only safety net during the talk — print or
    have on a second screen.

---

# Per-slide prompts & cheat sheet

# Slide 1 — Title

## Prompts

- Open with the one-sentence framing: "I built a quantitative platelet
  calcium model from scratch over ~6 months by repurposing an *E. coli*
  whole-cell modelling framework — and I want to show you both the
  methodology and a story about where it earned its keep."
- Set expectations: *what I built (slide 7)*, *how I built it
  (slides 3–6)*, *the moment the method paid off (slide 8)*,
  *validation (9–11)*, *what's next (12)*.

## Cheat sheet

- **Whole-cell model** — a mechanistic model of every known process in a
  single cell, with mass-balance and copy-number accounting. Distinct
  from a pathway model.
- **Dash lab** — host audience; calibrate jargon level to what they
  routinely see.

---

# Slide 2 — Framework choice

## Prompts

- Be candid about the cost-benefit: you didn't write a simulation engine
  from scratch; you inherited ~5,000 lines of validated code (state
  partitioning, listener / analysis framework) by forking wcEcoli.
- Pruning *E. coli* biology took ~1 month — orders of magnitude less
  than building an engine.
- Anticipate: *"Why not SBML?"* → SBML is a file format and BioModels is a
  catalogue; neither gives compartmentalised mass balance across coupled
  processes. *"Why not COPASI or V-Cell?"* → great single-pathway ODE
  tools, no copy-number accounting across compartments.
- Land the Prof. Covert email — keeps it human.

## Cheat sheet

- **COPASI** — open-source biochemical pathway simulator; great for
  single-pathway ODE work.
- **V-Cell** — UConn cell modelling platform; spatial PDE-capable,
  domain-specific.
- **SBML** — Systems Biology Markup Language; XML interchange format.
- **BioModels** — EBI repository of curated SBML models.
- **wcEcoli** — Covert Lab's whole-cell *E. coli* model (Karr 2012
  lineage; Macklin / Sun *Science* 2020).
- **State partitioning** — how a multi-process simulator divides shared
  molecule pools (e.g. ATP) between competing processes each timestep.
- **Mass balance** — every atom of every species accounted for, with
  compartment-aware bookkeeping.

---

# Slide 3 — Methodology

## Prompts

- "I'm a biologist by training. I want to show how a biologist builds a
  calibrated model — what the loop looks like when you don't have a
  software engineer's reflexes."
- Stress that every numerical value carries a primary-source citation
  comment in the code. This is the discipline that catches transcription
  errors (segues into slide 8).
- "Step 5 is the painful one. There are ~85 rate constants in this
  model; each is a small research project."

## Cheat sheet

- **Calibrated model** — a model whose parameters are constrained by
  measurements rather than fitted to the validation experiment.
- **Anchor paper** — the single paper whose experiment you'll validate
  against; defines targets up front.
- **IP3R** — inositol-1,4,5-trisphosphate receptor; Ca²⁺ release
  channel on the DTS.
- **SERCA** — sarco/endoplasmic-reticulum Ca²⁺ ATPase; pumps Ca²⁺ back
  into the DTS.
- **PMCA** — plasma-membrane Ca²⁺ ATPase; pumps Ca²⁺ out of the cell.
- **SOCE** — store-operated Ca²⁺ entry; STIM1 / Orai1 mechanism that
  refills depleted stores from extracellular Ca²⁺.
- **DTS** — dense tubular system; platelet equivalent of ER, the
  intracellular Ca²⁺ store.
- **Burkhart 2012** — platelet proteomics dataset; absolute copy
  numbers for ~3,800 platelet proteins.

---

# Slide 4 — Build code from source data (excerpt)

## Prompts

- This slide is almost entirely visual — point at the source PDF table
  on screen and say "this is what step 2 of the method looks like in
  practice."
- One sentence and move on; the *story* lands on slide 5.

## Cheat sheet

- *(visual slide — terms repeat from slide 3)*

---

# Slide 5 — Ask AI to extract

## Prompts

- "I gave Claude the line: *please read Dolan and Diamond 2014 and tell
  me what the first 5 lines of Table 1 show.*" Demo the kind of output.
- Frame the division of labour: AI does the rote PDF extraction; you do
  the sanity check.
- Headline claim: 100s of papers a day is reachable; the bottleneck is
  verification — and verification is also AI-amenable (see slide 8).
- *Don't overclaim authorship of the model itself.*

## Cheat sheet

- **PDF extraction** — historically painful for tables and footnotes;
  modern multimodal LLMs are now reliable on well-formatted tables, less
  so on figures.
- **Sanity check** — the human step: does the extracted value match the
  paper's text? Do the units make sense?

---

# Slide 6 — AI to build the code

## Prompts

- Be specific about what "building the code" means: scaffolding new
  process modules off the wcEcoli template, wiring rate constants into
  the existing solver, writing unit tests for invariants.
- Concede the architecture honestly: "works well, not perfect, will
  need refactoring before v0.5" — pre-empts critique and signals
  engineering self-awareness.
- Anticipate: *"Did the AI write code that worked first try?"* → no, the
  back-and-forth is iterative; the AI is a fast scaffolder and a useful
  pair-programmer, not an oracle.

## Cheat sheet

- **wcEcoli template** — the reference architecture for processes,
  states, listeners and analysis plots. Subclassing the framework's
  abstract base classes is the path of least resistance.

---

# Slide 7 — Pathway diagram (what I built)

## Prompts

- Stand back and trace one signal path through the diagram once:
  *thrombin → PAR1/PAR4 → Gαq → PLCβ → IP₃ → IP3R → cytosolic Ca²⁺
  peak*.
- Point at the boxes labelled "(not yet in model)" — TXA₂R, P2Y₁₂, PKC,
  MCU — and frame them as known scope cuts, not omissions.
- Don't enumerate all twelve mechanisms; let the figure carry the rest.

## Cheat sheet

- **Agonist** — a signal molecule that activates a platelet (thrombin,
  ADP, TXA₂).
- **GPCR** — G-protein-coupled receptor (PAR1/PAR4 for thrombin; P2Y1
  and P2Y12 for ADP).
- **Gαq** — α-subunit of the heterotrimeric G-protein that activates
  PLCβ. **Gᵢ** (P2Y12) is the inhibitory counterpart.
- **PLCβ** — phospholipase Cβ; cleaves PIP₂ → IP₃ + DAG.
- **IP₃** — inositol-1,4,5-trisphosphate; the second messenger that
  gates IP3R.
- **NCX / NCLX / MCU** — Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger (PM); Na⁺/Ca²⁺/Li⁺
  exchanger (mitochondrial); mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uniporter.
- **CaM** — calmodulin; cytosolic Ca²⁺ sensor / buffer; activates PMCA
  at high Ca²⁺.
- **CALR / BiP / HSP90B1 / CREC** — major intra-DTS Ca²⁺-binding
  buffers.
- **STIM1 / Orai1** — DTS Ca²⁺ sensor + PM channel; the SOCE
  machinery.
- **P2X1** — ATP-gated PM Ca²⁺ channel; ionotropic, not GPCR.
- **SCS / OCS** — surface-connected (open canalicular) system; the
  platelet's invaginated extracellular surface.
- **TXA₂R** — thromboxane A₂ receptor; not yet in model.
- **PKC** — protein kinase C; not yet in model.

---

# Slide 8 — Spotting errors in the source (k₃)

## Prompts

- This is the storytelling slide. Slow down.
- "I asked the AI to cross-check the values it had extracted. It
  flagged one — k₃ on the IP3R — as 100× the value in the primary
  source. I went back to the primary, confirmed the typo, corrected
  the value, and the resting state stabilised."
- Land the moral: **AI flagged the anomaly; the primary-source
  citation discipline made the verification cheap.** Either alone is
  weaker.
- ⚠ **Verify the citation** — the lab-book audit ties this to
  Sneyd-Dufour 2002, not Purvis 2008. Don't misattribute on a slide.

## Cheat sheet

- **k₃** — third rate constant in the IP3R gating model; controls the
  rate of an inhibitory binding transition. Wrong value → wrong gating
  → wrong steady state.
- **Sneyd-Dufour 2002** — IP3R Markov-state model.
- **Purvis 2008** — earlier platelet calcium model by Purvis,
  Chatterjee, Brass and Diamond (*Biophys. J.*).
- **Resting state** — the steady state the model settles to when left
  un-stimulated; the load-bearing sanity check.

---

# Slide 9 — Validation

## Prompts

- Frame: "These criteria were defined *before* I ran the validation.
  Pass/fail, no post-hoc adjustment."
- Walk the ✓ list — four resting-state targets all hit. Twenty-one of
  twenty-one unit tests pass. Driven by **physiological agonist
  concentrations** (1 nM thrombin, 10 µM ADP) — emphasise: no
  hand-fitted IP₃.
- Anticipate: *"Why isn't this overfitting?"* → rate constants come from
  independent primary sources; the only tuned knob is initial copy
  number, all within Burkhart 2012 ranges.
- **Drop "arbitrary" from the slide before presenting** — use
  "pre-specified" or "biological-range".

## Cheat sheet

- **Resting state targets** — cytosolic Ca²⁺ in 90–110 nM; DTS Ca²⁺
  in literature range (100–400 µM); IP₃ ≈ 50 nM.
- **IP₃-stimulated transient** — the calcium spike following IP₃
  release from the GPCR cascade.
- **Gαq-active** — fraction of Gαq protein in its GTP-bound, active
  conformation; an upstream proxy for receptor occupancy.
- **Unit test** — automated invariant check (e.g. mass balance,
  positivity, sub-state sum).

---

# Slide 10 — Cytosolic Ca²⁺ trace

## Prompts

- Headline number: cytosolic Ca²⁺ rises ~100 nM → 500 nM — a ~5×
  transient on a sub-micromolar background.
- Point at the rise time (~15–20 s, matches Dolan timing) and the
  return-to-baseline.
- "Driven only by 1 nM thrombin + 10 µM ADP at *t* = 0 — no other
  inputs."
- **Fix the typo on the slide: "Cytoslic" → "Cytosolic".**

## Cheat sheet

- **Transient peak height** — maximum cytosolic Ca²⁺ during the spike,
  one of the validation criteria.
- **Free Ca²⁺** — the ~1 % of cytosolic Ca²⁺ not bound to a buffer at
  any given moment; what fluorescent indicators report.

---

# Slide 11 — DTS Ca²⁺ trace

## Prompts

- DTS depletes ~60 % during the transient and refills via SOCE on the
  back side.
- "If you remove extracellular Ca²⁺ (EDTA condition), DTS refilling
  fails — that's the paired ± Ca²⁺ experiment from Dolan Fig. 4, and
  the model reproduces it."
- "Most published platelet models report only free cytosolic Ca²⁺.
  Whole-cell accounting lets me show the store dynamics directly."

## Cheat sheet

- **DTS depletion** — the fraction of the DTS Ca²⁺ pool released into
  cytosol during the transient.
- **SOCE refill** — STIM1 detects low DTS Ca²⁺, oligomerises, recruits
  Orai1 at the PM, and Ca²⁺ flows from extracellular space back into
  the DTS via cytosol.
- **EDTA condition** — paired experiment with extracellular Ca²⁺
  chelated; isolates the SOCE contribution.

---

# Slide 12 — Outlook

## Prompts

- Near-term technical: granule release (v0.5 — lets the peak *do*
  something); fix the long-recovery cytosolic Ca²⁺ collapse
  (deYoung-Keizer bistability artefact, known); add P2Y₁₂ and TXA₂R.
- *Experimental* application: add clopidogrel to the model and predict
  the platelet response. Strong audience hook.
- Wider impact: platelet–CTC coupling (relevant to the Dash lab's
  interests); thrombus formation as a multi-cell extension.
- Close on methodology generalisation: "The five-step process I showed
  on slide 3 is the contribution as much as the model itself."
- **Consider rewriting "Long-recovery cytosolic Ca²⁺ collapse — a
  limitation of modelling perhaps?"** Own it: "a known artefact of
  the deYoung-Keizer IP3R model under sustained stimulation;
  characterised, fixable in v0.5."

## Cheat sheet

- **Granule release / exocytosis** — Ca²⁺-triggered fusion of dense
  and α-granules with the PM, dumping ADP, serotonin, fibrinogen,
  P-selectin.
- **P2Y₁₂** — inhibitory ADP receptor (Gᵢ); molecular target of
  clopidogrel (Plavix).
- **TXA₂R** — thromboxane A₂ receptor; the aspirin-pathway target.
- **Clopidogrel** — antiplatelet drug, P2Y₁₂ antagonist; standard
  post-stent care.
- **deYoung-Keizer bistability** — known artefact of the deYoung-Keizer
  IP3R model under prolonged stimulation; the channel can lock into a
  low-flux state.
- **CTC** — circulating tumour cell; metastasis-relevant cell type
  that interacts with platelets.
- **Thrombus** — platelet–fibrin aggregate; clot.

---

# Prioritised fix list

1. *(must)* Slide 9 — replace "arbitrary" with "pre-specified" or
   "biological-range".
2. *(must)* Slide 10 — fix "Cytoslic" → "Cytosolic".
3. *(must)* Slide 8 — verify citation (Sneyd-Dufour 2002 vs Purvis
   2008) before the talk.
4. *(should)* Slide 1 — strengthen the title beyond "feasibility".
5. *(should)* Slide 8 — reconsider "An aside" framing; this story is
   load-bearing.
6. *(should)* Slide 12 — own the long-recovery collapse rather than
   posing it as a question.
7. *(nice-to-have)* Consider reordering: 1, 2, 7 (what), 3 (how),
   8 (story), 9–11 (results), 12 (outlook).
8. *(nice-to-have)* Slide 7 — fix "BIP" → "BiP".
